---
title: "About the MMLoS Project"
description-meta: "Project overview, goals, and context for the Multi-Modal Level of Service research"
---

# Project Overview

The **Multi-Modal Level of Service (MMLoS)** project is a research programme commissioned by **Active Travel England (ATE)** to develop a new measurement framework and proof-of-concept tool for evaluating how streets support active and sustainable travel modes.

## The Problem

Transport appraisal historically focuses on **motor vehicle Level of Service (LOS)**—measures like delay, speed, and volume-to-capacity ratio. These metrics dominate investment decisions despite failing to capture the user experience for:

- **Pedestrians** — quality of footways, crossing delays, perceived safety
- **Cyclists** — lane width, separation from traffic, junction treatment
- **Public transport users** — service frequency, stop amenities, reliability
- **Wheelchair users** — pavement quality, dropped kerbs, gradients

When each mode is assessed separately—as current practice does—it becomes difficult to answer a fundamental planning question: *how do design choices trade off across user groups*? A wider bus lane might improve transit LOS but worsen cycling and pedestrian LOS. A raised zebra crossing aids pedestrians but may increase vehicle delay.Decision-makers need a tool that places these impacts on a **common scale**.

## Our Approach

### Phase 1: Literature & Synthesis (Completed Q1 2026)
Reviewed existing MMLoS frameworks, including:
- US FDOT/NCHRP multimodal LOS models [@guttenplan2001multimodal; @nchrp616]
- Highway Capacity Manual multimodal methods [@highway_capacity_manual_7]
- Complete Streets performance measurement [@nacto2016street]
- UK ATE scheme review tools and WebTAG appraisal framework

[See the full literature review →](/research.qmd)

### Phase 2: Methodology Development (2026)
Building on the review, we will:

1. **Identify common attributes** across modes (e.g., effective width, user density, delay)
2. **Calibrate common scales** that allow cross-modal comparison (using stated preference research where available)
3. **Integrate mode-specific attributes** without breaking commensurability
4. **Design junction-level models** that link network connectivity to user experience

### Phase 3: Data Integration & Tooling (2026–2027)
Leverage open and accessible data sources:

- **OpenStreetMap** — network topology, infrastructure tags (cycleways, crossings, surfaces)
- **Street-level imagery** (where available) — automated geometry extraction via computer vision / image-to-3D
- **Transport datasets** — STATS19 collisions, Bus Open Data Service (BODS), DfT traffic counts
- **Case study ground-truthing** — local partner data from Leeds, Essex, West Midlands

We will produce a **web-based tool** that:
- Accepts a location (address or map click)
- Computes MMLoS scores for nearby links and junctions
- Visualises trade-offs across modes
- Supports scenario testing (e.g., "what if a bus lane were added?")

### Phase 4: Validation & Documentation (2027)
- Proof-of-concept testing across three case-study areas
- User evaluation with local authority partners
- Full documentation for ATE integration and future development

## Project Timeline

| Phase | Period | Key Milestones |
|-------|--------|----------------|
| **Setup & Lit Review** | Apr–Jul 2026 | Literature review report, methodology white paper |
| **Method Development** | Aug 2026–Jan 2027 | Tool structure specification, calibration dataset |
| **Implementation** | Feb–Jun 2027 | Working web tool, case study results |
| **Validation & Delivery** | Jul–Sep 2027 | Final report, documentation, handover to ATE |

The project start date is **1 April 2026**. All outputs will be published as open-source where possible.

## Research Team

- **Principal Investigator:** Dr Tom Cohen (University of Westminster, Active Travel Academy)
- **Co-Investigator:** Prof Robin Lovelace (University of Leeds, Institute for Transport Studies)
- **Research Assistant:** [To be recruited – Westminster]
- **Research Software Engineer:** [To be recruited]
- **Research Fellow / Stakeholder Lead:** [To be recruited]

**Subcontractors:**
- **IFP-r** (International Federation of Pedestrians – research branch): 3D LiDAR scanning and point cloud analysis
- **Design Advisory Group** (pro bono): Rachel Aldred, Peter White, Jamie Furlong

## Funders & Partners

This project is funded by **Active Travel England** as part of its research programme to improve tools and guidance for active travel scheme design and appraisal.

We are grateful to our **Steering Group** partners—including local authorities from Essex, West Midlands, and Leeds—for their guidance and data access.

## Outputs

1. **MMLoS method specification** — methodological report detailing scoring functions and data requirements
2. **Proof-of-concept web tool** — usable by ATE and pilot local authorities
3. **Peer-reviewed paper(s)** — detailing methodology and findings
4. **Open-source code** — hosted under the [multilevelos GitHub organisation](https://github.com/multilevelos)
5. **Documentation and guidance** — for tool use and further development

---

*This page provides an overview. For technical details, see the [Research](/research.qmd) section. For status updates, see [Updates](/updates.qmd).*
